# Candle portable (run install-candle.ps1 locally)
candle-11.2/
candle-*-portable.zip
*.zip

# Cursor / IDE
.cursor/

# Fox Alien Masuter (GRBL) automation

Portable helpers for **Fox Alien Masuter-class** routers running **GRBL 1.x** over **USB serial** (often CH341/CH340), plus **dry-run / air-carve** sample G-code.

## Prerequisites

1. Install **Microsoft Windows** PowerShell **5.x** or **PowerShell Core** (`System.IO.Ports`).
2. USB driver for CH340 from WCH if no COM port appears.
3. **Candle**: run `install-candle.ps1` (downloads Candle 11.2 portable beside this repo) or point `Run-Candle-Masuter.bat` at your install.

Operational notes, COM quirks, limit-switch behavior: see **`masuter-setup.txt`**.

## Scripts

| Script | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| `Disconnect-Candle.ps1` | Stop Candle so another process can open the serial port |
| `Run-Candle-Masuter.bat` | Launch Candle pointing at `./candle-11.2/Candle/` |
| `install-candle.ps1` | Download and extract Candle 11.2 portable |
| `DryRun-WebCircle.ps1` | Stream a `.nc` file line-by-line to GRBL (default: Feather circle dry path) |
| `Move-To-Table-Center.ps1` | G53 XY move toward table centre from EEPROM `$130`/`$131` |
| `Move-To-Z-Top.ps1` | G53 Z move to configured homed-top machine coordinate |
| `Send-HOME.ps1`, `Probe-Z-Deck.ps1`, `Spindle-On.ps1` | Machine-specific utilities (verify wiring before relying on them) |
| `New-ComplexDryRun.ps1` | Generate a dense hypotrochoid air path (`samples/complex-spirograph-air-dry.nc`-style) |

Set **`MASUTER_COM`** (for example `COM7`) before scripts that take `-Com`.

## Samples

Under **`samples/`**: box trace, feather circle (MIT), synthetic Z-clamped wave, generated spirograph. Third-party attribution: **`samples/from-web/SOURCE_AND_LICENSE.txt`**.

## Safety

Dry-run NC files intend **fixed safe Z** above the spoilboard **and** spindle off unless you deliberately change code. Confirm machine coordinates, feeds, and limit wiring before unattended runs.

## Origin

Previously lived under another workspace (`MEDISOFT` monorepo). This repo is now the standalone home for this tooling.

Under **`samples/`**: box trace, feather circle (MIT), synthetic Z-clamped wave, generated spirograph. Third-party attribution: **`samples/from-web/SOURCE_AND_LICENSE.txt`**.

## Safety

Dry-run NC files intend **fixed safe Z** above the spoilboard **and** spindle off unless you deliberately change code. Confirm machine coordinates, feeds, and limit wiring before unattended runs.

## Repository

This project is maintained as its **own Git repository** at [`SEAS2025/FoxAlienMasuter`](https://github.com/SEAS2025/FoxAlienMasuter). Earlier snapshots may also appear in other workspaces; treat **this clone** as canonical.

content.text:
The live article page did not return full step text here, but the project matches the standard **terrain STL → Easel Pro → CNC** pattern Inventables documents as well ([Inventables project mirror](https://www.inventables.com/projects/topographic-surface-and-carving-with-easel)). Below is how that workflow works and how it relates to your **Masuter + GRBL/Candle** setup.

---

### What the Instructable is teaching

The core idea in guides like [Topographic Surface Download and CNC Carving With Easel Pro Software](https://www.instructables.com/Topographic-Surface-Download-and-CNC-Carving-With-/):

1. **Get 3D terrain for an area**  
   Common sources (often referenced alongside Easel tutorials):
   - **[TouchTerrain](https://touchterrain.geol.iastate.edu/main)** (Iowa State): draw a box on the map, export **STL** (often binary), unzip.
   - **Equator** / other providers: **printable mesh / elevation** as **STL** (account + project flow varies).

2. **Open Easel Pro (Inventables)**  
   Easel Pro adds **3D / STL import** and carving setup vs free Easel.

3. **Import the STL**  
   Set **material size**, **bit**, **feeds/speeds**, and often **vertical exaggeration** (terrain is flat in real life; exaggeration makes hills readable on a plaque).

4. **Generate toolpaths**  
   Roughing + finishing passes typical for **relief / 3D carve** (ball end or small flat end for finishing).

5. **Machine**  
   The original write-up targets **Inventables / X-Carve + Easel** as sender.

There is also a **video walkthrough** linked from search results: [YouTube — Topographic Surface Download and CNC Carving with Easel Pro Software](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xYdXevYavt4).

---

### Your setup (Masuter, Candle, GRBL)

**Easel is built around Inventables machines.** For a non–X-Carve GRBL router you usually:

- Still use **TouchTerrain (or similar)** to get the **STL**, then  
- Use **CAM that outputs GRBL-friendly G-code** (Fusion 360, Carbide Create + export, MeshCAM, FreeCAD CAM, etc.), **or**  
- Use Easel only if you can **export / post-process G-code** for your controller (check current Easel export options for your account).

So: **learn the terrain-download + 3D-relief concepts from the Instructable**, but plan **final G-code** for **GRBL + Candle** (or another sender), not only Easel’s default carve-to-machine path.

---

### Practical checklist (conceptually matching the article)

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Pick map bounds (same idea as “recenter box” in TouchTerrain). |
| 2 | Export **STL**; note units (mm vs inches). |
| 3 | Decide **stock** thickness and **max depth** of carve (terrain scaled to fit). |
| 4 | Set **vertical exaggeration** in CAM (or in TouchTerrain if offered). |
| 5 | Choose **bit** (e.g. rough with flat endmill, finish with ball nose). |
| 6 | **Dry-run / air-cut** at safe Z (your repo already does machine-safe dry paths). |
| 7 | Cut with feeds appropriate for **wood** and **machine rigidity**. |

---

### Related deeper dive

For a more manual terrain pipeline (mesh prep, fusion, etc.), another Instructables line is [Carving 3D Terrain Topographic Relief Maps on a CNC Machine](https://www.instructables.com/Carving-3D-Terrain-Topographic-Relief-Maps-on-a-CN/) — useful if you outgrow “one STL from one web app.”

If you tell me your preferred CAM (**Fusion**, **Carbide**, **MeshCAM**, or trying **Easel export**), I can outline the exact click-path from STL → G-code → Candle on the Masuter without Easel lock-in.

[REDACTED]

$nf = Join-Path $env:TEMP '_sm.nc'
$d = @{rx=21; ry=12; cx=0; cy=-13; segs=40}
$m = '; smiley shallow carve - G54 Z0 TOP of stock (25 mm pine) face depth 2 mm
; Tool: ideally 3-4 mm carbide TWO FLUTE DOWNCUT preferred for pine fuzz
; Assumes spindle CW M3 typical for woodworking router
;
; BEFORE RUN: Clamp board. Candle COM 115200. Home ($H). Jog spindle over BOARD CENTER.
; ZERO G54 XY at board heart center (not table center unless board is centered there).
; ZERO G54 Z on TOP surface of pine (paper-snatch or probe). Z+ is UP.
;
; Estimated max DOC this file approx 2.0 mm inside top surface.
';
$lines=@()
$lines+='G21'
$lines+='G17'
$lines+='G90'
$lines+='G94'
$lines+="G54"
$lines+="M5"
$lines+="G4 P2"
$lines+="M3 S7200"
$lines+="G4 P4"
$lines+="G0 Z10"
$cR=34.0;$safeZ=12;$appZ=1
$fz=380;$pz=115
$tpi=[math]::PI
# Outer face CCW dia 68 (R34)
$x0,$y0=[math]::Round($cR,3),0.0
$lines+="G0 X$x0 Y$y0"
$lines+="G1 Z${appZ} F600"
$lines+="G1 Z-2.0 F$pz"
$lines+="G3 X$x0 Y$y0 I{0} J0 F$fz" -f (-$cR)
$lines+="G0 Z${safeZ}"
# Eyes centers path R eyeR
$eyeR=5
foreach($cen in @(,-12.5,14),'r12.5,14')){
  ; # fix syntax
}
input.description: (Aborted draft - will use Write directly)

input.contents:
param(
  [string]$OutFile = $(Join-Path $PSScriptRoot 'samples/smiley-face-soft-pine-G54.mm.nc'),
  [double]$DepthMm = -2.0,
  [double]$FaceRadiusMm = 34.0,
  [int]$FeedXY = 380,
  [int]$PlungeZFeed = 110,
  [int]$SpinRpm = 7200
)

$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'

$zm = '{0:G7}' -f $DepthMm
$negI = '-' + ([string]$FaceRadiusMm).TrimEnd('0').TrimEnd('.')

$L = New-Object System.Collections.Generic.List[string]
[void]$L.Add('; smiley_face_soft_pine G54 XY board center Z TOP of pine spindle ON')
[void]$L.Add('; Stock soft pine nominally 25 mm. G54 Z0 = TOP face. Deepest Z in this job is ' + $zm + ' mm')
[void]$L.Add('; CLAMP BOARD. E_STOP ready. Vacuum if you have one.')
[void]$L.Add('; AFTER $H jog over BOARD CENTER: Set XY zero in Candle. Probe/touch TOP set Z zero')
[void]$L.Add('; Tool: 3 mm or so two-flute down-cut works well on pine')
$L.Add('')
$L.Add('G21')
$L.Add('G17')
$L.Add('G90')
$L.Add('G94')
$L.Add('G54')
$L.Add('')
$L.Add('M5')
$L.Add('G4 P2')
$L.Add(('M3 S{0}' -f $SpinRpm))
$L.Add('G4 P4')
$L.Add('')
$L.Add('G0 Z10')

$L.Add(('G0 X{0} Y0.0' -f ([string]$FaceRadiusMm)))

$L.Add('G1 Z1.0 F800')
$L.Add(('G1 Z{0} F{1}' -f $zm, $PlungeZFeed))
$L.Add(('G3 X{0} Y0.0 {1} J0 F{2}' -f ([string]$FaceRadiusMm), ('I-{0}' -f ([string]$FaceRadiusMm)), $FeedXY))

$L.Add('')
$L.Add('G0 Z10')

$eyes = @(
  @{ Cx=-12.5; Cy=13.5; R=5.0 },
  @{ Cx=12.5;  Cy=13.5; R=5.0 }
)
foreach ($e in $eyes) {
  $xs = $e.Cx + $e.R
  $L.Add(('G0 X{0} Y{1}' -f ([string]$xs).Replace(',', '.'), ([string]$e.Cy)))
  $L.Add('G1 Z1 F800')
  $L.Add(('G1 Z{0} F{1}' -f $zm, $PlungeZFeed))
  $L.Add(('G3 X{0} Y{1} I-{2} J0 F{3}' -f ([string]$xs), ([string]$e.Cy), ([string]$e.R), $FeedXY))
  $L.Add('')
  $L.Add('G0 Z10')
}

$tpi = [math]::PI

$first = $true
for ($si = 0; $si -le $mouthSeg; $si++) {
  $t = $tpi + ($si / [double]$mouthSeg) * $tpi
  $mx = [math]::Round($mouthRx * [math]::Cos($t), 3)
  $my = [math]::Round(($mouthCy + $mouthRy * [math]::Sin($t)), 3)
  if ($first) {
    $first = $false
    $L.Add(('G0 X{0} Y{1}' -f $mx.ToString([System.Globalization.CultureInfo]::InvariantCulture), $my.ToString([System.Globalization.CultureInfo]::InvariantCulture)))
    $L.Add('G1 Z1 F800')
    $L.Add(('G1 Z{0} F{1}' -f $zm, $PlungeZFeed))
  } else {
    $L.Add(('G1 X{0} Y{1} F{2}' -f $mx.ToString([System.Globalization.CultureInfo]::InvariantCulture), $my.ToString([System.Globalization.CultureInfo]::InvariantCulture), $FeedXY))
  }
}

$L.Add('')
$L.Add('G0 Z10')
$L.Add('G0 X0 Y0')
$L.Add('M5')
$L.Add('G4 P3')
$L.Add('')
$L.Add('; end smiley carve')

[System.IO.File]::WriteAllLines($OutFile, $L.ToArray(), [System.Text.UTF8Encoding]::new($false))
Write-Host "Wrote $OutFile lines=$($L.Count)"

param(
  [string]$OutFile = $(Join-Path $PSScriptRoot 'samples/smiley-face-soft-pine-G54.mm.nc'),
  [double]$DepthMm = -2.0,
  [double]$FaceRadiusMm = 34.0,
  [double]$EyeCenterY = 13.5,
  [double]$EyePathRadius = 5.0,
  [double]$MouthCx = 0.0,
  [double]$MouthCy = -12.0,
  [double]$MouthRx = 21.0,
  [double]$MouthRy = 11.0,
  [int]$MouthSeg = 42,
  [int]$FeedXY = 380,
  [int]$PlungeZFeed = 110,
  [int]$SpinRpm = 7200
)

$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'
$culture = [System.Globalization.CultureInfo]::InvariantCulture
function FNum([double]$v) {
  '{0:R}' -f $v | ForEach-Object { $_ }
}

$L = New-Object System.Collections.Generic.List[string]
[void]$L.Add('; smiley_face_soft_pine G54 XY board center Z TOP of pine spindle ON')
[void]$L.Add(('; Soft pine nominally 25 mm. G54 Z0 = TOP surface. Deepest cut Z = {0} mm' -f (FNum $DepthMm)))
[void]$L.Add('; CLAMP BOARD. KEEP CLEAR. E_STOP ready.')
[void]$L.Add('; AFTER homing jog over BOARD geometric center THEN zero G54 XY in Candle.')
[void]$L.Add('; Zero G54 Z on TOP of pine thin-paper-drag or jog down until scratch then Z0.')
[void]$L.Add('; Tool suggestion: approx 3 mm two-flute down-cut for fuzz control on pine')
$L.Add('')
$L.Add('G21')
$L.Add('G17')
$L.Add('G90')
$L.Add('G94')
$L.Add('G54')
$L.Add('')
$L.Add('M5')
$L.Add('G4 P2')
$L.Add(('M3 S{0}' -f $SpinRpm))
$L.Add('G4 P4')
$L.Add('')
$L.Add('G0 Z10')

$fr = [double]$FaceRadiusMm
$L.Add(('G0 X{0} Y{1}' -f (FNum $fr), (FNum 0.0)))

$L.Add('G1 Z1.0 F800')
$L.Add(('G1 Z{0} F{1}' -f ((FNum $DepthMm)), $PlungeZFeed))
$L.Add(('G3 X{0} Y{1} I{2} J0 F{3}' -f (FNum $fr), (FNum 0), (FNum (-$fr)), $FeedXY))

$L.Add('')
$L.Add('G0 Z10')

$eyes = @(
  @{ Cx=-12.5; Cy=$EyeCenterY; R=$EyePathRadius },
  @{ Cx=12.5;  Cy=$EyeCenterY; R=$EyePathRadius }
)

foreach ($e in $eyes) {
  [double]$xs = $e.Cx + $e.R
  $L.Add(('G0 X{0} Y{1}' -f (FNum $xs), (FNum $e.Cy)))
  $L.Add('G1 Z1 F800')
  $L.Add(('G1 Z{0} F{1}' -f (FNum $DepthMm), $PlungeZFeed))
  $L.Add(('G3 X{0} Y{1} I{2} J0 F{3}' -f (FNum $xs), (FNum $e.Cy), (FNum (-$e.R)), $FeedXY))
  $L.Add('')
  $L.Add('G0 Z10')
}

$tpi = [math]::PI

$first = $true

for ($si = 0; $si -le $MouthSeg; $si++) {
  [double]$t = $tpi + ($si / [double]$MouthSeg) * $tpi
  [double]$mx = [math]::Round(($MouthRx * [math]::Cos($t)), 4)
  [double]$my = [math]::Round(($MouthCy + $MouthRy * [math]::Sin($t)), 4)

  if ($first) {
    $first = $false
    $L.Add(('G0 X{0} Y{1}' -f (FNum $mx), (FNum $my)))
    $L.Add('G1 Z1 F800')
    $L.Add(('G1 Z{0} F{1}' -f (FNum $DepthMm), $PlungeZFeed))
  }
  else {
    $L.Add(('G1 X{0} Y{1} F{2}' -f (FNum $mx), (FNum $my), $FeedXY))
  }
}

$L.Add('')
$L.Add('G0 Z10')
$L.Add('G0 X0 Y0')
$L.Add('M5')
$L.Add('G4 P3')
$L.Add('')
$L.Add('; end smiley carve')

[System.IO.File]::WriteAllLines($OutFile, $L.ToArray(), [System.Text.UTF8Encoding]::new($false))

Write-Host "Wrote $OutFile lines=$($L.Count)"

7) PowerShell one-liners: avoid naming helpers `R` (and similar)
   PowerShell binds `R` to the `Invoke-History` alias; calls like `(R $port 5000)` can error with "positional parameter … Invoke-History". Use spelled-out names (`ReadFor`, `Send-Line`, etc.).

8) Shallow smiley carve into soft pine (`samples/smiley-face-soft-pine-G54.mm.nc`)
   Uses G54: Z0 MUST be TOP of stock; XY origin at board centre beneath the spindle.
   Typical flow: `$H`; Run-Smiley-Carve.ps1 -OnlyCenterTable (disconnect Candle) for machine table-centre XY; jog bit over VISUAL pine centre → zero XY in Candle;
   jog Z gently to touch TOP of pine thin-paper-drag → zero Z there; CLOSE Candle; rerun Run-Smiley-Carve.ps1 → type CUT to stream spindle job (closes after ~minute of slow moves).
   Regenerate contour with New-SmileyFacePineNc.ps1 (adjust -DepthMm, -FeedXY). Default cutter assumption ~3 mm; edit NC if kerf visibly wrong.

content.text:
### Table center (just ran)

`Move-To-Table-Center.ps1` completed on **COM7** (CH340 autosense):

- **Command:** `G21 G90 G53 G0 X-200 Y-190 F4500`
- **After:** `<Idle|MPos:-200,-190,-1>`

That is **table midpoint in machine space**, not necessarily the geometric centre **of your pine board**. Jog slightly if the bit is not above the dot you want, then zero **XY in Candle** where the smile sits.

---

### Smile carve I added (repo **`FoxAlienMasuter`**)

| Item | Role |
|--------|-----|
| `samples/smiley-face-soft-pine-G54.mm.nc` | **G54** job: Ø68 mm face (~**2 mm deep** max), eyes, mouth polygon; **`M3 S7200`**; **~380 mm/min** XY, **110 mm/min** plunge |
| `New-SmileyFacePineNc.ps1` | Regenerate/adjust **`DepthMm`, `FeedXY`, etc.** |
| `Run-Smiley-Carve.ps1` | Runs table-centre move, prompts you, then optionally streams (**type `CUT`** = **spindle on**) |

Pushed **`62e2f59`** → [github.com/SEAS2025/FoxAlienMasuter](https://github.com/SEAS2025/FoxAlienMasuter).

Approximate carve section:

```21:29:c:/Users/User/Desktop/FoxAlienMasuter/samples/smiley-face-soft-pine-G54.mm.nc
G1 Z1.0 F800
G1 Z-2 F110
G3 X34 Y0 I-34 J0 F380

G0 Z10
G0 X-7.5 Y13.5
G1 Z1 F800
G1 Z-2 F110
```

---

### Before you carve (critical)

1. **Clamp** the 25 mm pine securely; clearance for **rapid Z 10 mm** (+Z upward from surface).
2. In **Candle** (recommended for first cut): reconnect, **jog** until the spindle is exactly over the **middle of the emoji on the wood**, hit **XY zero**.
3. **Touch-off Z** with paper or jog-scratch **on TOP of pine** → **Z zero** (positive Z retracts upward).
4. **Preview** [`samples/smiley-face-soft-pine-G54.mm.nc`](https://github.com/SEAS2025/FoxAlienMasuter/blob/main/samples/smiley-face-soft-pine-G54.mm.nc) bounds vs your clamps.
5. **Close Candle** before streaming from PowerShell, or run entirely from Candle’s sender if you prefer.
6. **Tool:** tuned for roughly **ø3 mm** cutter; spindle **7200 RPM** in file—raise/lower RPM in Candle or edit `SpinRpm` generator param if chatter or burning.

PowerShell carve path (unsafe over remote control; **you run this at the machine**):

```powershell
cd $HOME\Desktop\FoxAlienMasuter
powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File .\Run-Smiley-Carve.ps1
```

Use **`-OnlyCenterTable`** after you already zeroed XY/Z inside Candle:

```powershell
.\Run-Smiley-Carve.ps1 -OnlyCenterTable:$false    # omit -OnlyCenterTable for centre + CUT prompt flow
.\Run-Smiley-Carve.ps1 -OnlyCenterTable          # XY centre only -- then zero in Candle, reconnect, rerun without this switch before CUT
```

I did **not** stream **`CUT`** from here—that would spin the spindle without confirming your **G54** zero on the pine.

---

### If you paste `CUT` remotely next time anyway

Assume **severe injury risk**. Only after **you** visually confirm spindle over board centre **and** touch-off Z matches top of pine (`masuter-setup.txt` §**8**).

GitHub CLI showed a device code (**0A5A-5E2B**) and [https://github.com/login/device](https://github.com/login/device), but the flow hit **“context deadline exceeded”** after ~15 minutes — the authorization step wasn’t finished in time, so **`delete_repo` was never granted**.

### What that means for you

  - run **`gh auth refresh -h github.com -s delete_repo`** again and complete the browser flow **within the prompt window**, or

### Follow-up from here

Nothing else is required unless you still want **`FoxAlienCNC`** removed from GitHub; your active project stays **`FoxAlienMasuter`**. If you retry `gh auth refresh`, start it when you can complete the login immediately so it doesn’t time out again.

content.text:
Here’s what was wrong and what we changed.

### The job already asked for spindle on
Your smiley file has **`M3 S7200`** before any cutting. So “with spindle running” was already the intent last time — it may **not sound** loud or **not ramp** immediately if PWM/VFD lags while **`G4 P4`** was too short.

### What I changed and ran
1. **Regenerated** `samples/smiley-face-soft-pine-G54.mm.nc` with **`G4 P12`** right after **`M3 S7200`** (12 s dwell so the spindle can come up before `G0`/`G1`).
2. **`DryRun-WebCircle.ps1`** default **`LineWaitMs`** raised to **90 s** so long **`G4`** lines don’t time out waiting for **`ok`**.
3. **Streamed the full job again:** **74 lines**, **`exit_code: 0`**, ended **`Idle`** / spindle off (**`FS:0,0`** after **`M5`**).

content.text:
Implementing multi-pass step-down (avoid plunging −7 mm at once — common cause of snags), safer retract height, and slightly lower spindle RPM.

[REDACTED]

Rewriting the generator to use **multi-pass step-down** (~2 mm layers to −7 mm), **taller retractions** (`G0 Z18`) between passes for chip/air clearance, **lower spindle RPM**, and slightly gentler feeds. Single plunges to −7 mm overload small bits.

[REDACTED]

input.contents:
param(
  [string]$OutFile = $(Join-Path $PSScriptRoot 'samples/smiley-face-soft-pine-G54.mm.nc'),
  [double]$DepthMm = -7.0,
  [double]$StepDownMm = 2.0,
  [double]$FaceRadiusMm = 34.0,
  [double]$EyeCenterY = 13.5,
  [double]$EyePathRadius = 5.0,
  [double]$MouthCx = 0.0,
  [double]$MouthCy = -12.0,
  [double]$MouthRx = 21.0,
  [double]$MouthRy = 11.0,
  [int]$MouthSeg = 42,
  [int]$FeedXY = 145,
  [int]$PlungeZFeed = 45,
  [int]$ApproachXYFeed = 220,
  [double]$SafeZMm = 18.0,
  [double]$SkinZMm = 1.0,
  [int]$SpinRpm = 6000,
  [int]$SpinupDwellSeconds = 12
)

$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'
if ($DepthMm -ge -0.0001) { throw 'DepthMm must be negative (below stock top Z0).' }
if ($StepDownMm -le 0.1) { throw 'StepDownMm must be positive (mm per slice).' }

$script:culture = [System.Globalization.CultureInfo]::InvariantCulture

function FNum([double]$v) {
  [string]::Format($script:culture, '{0:G7}', $v)
}

function Get-ZLevels([double]$TargetDepthNeg, [double]$StepDownPositive) {
  $ta = [math]::Abs($TargetDepthNeg)
  $acc = 0.0
  $list = New-Object System.Collections.Generic.List[double]
  while ($acc + 1e-9 -lt $ta) {
    $acc = [math]::Min($acc + $StepDownPositive, $ta)
    $list.Add(-$acc)
  }
  if ($list.Count -lt 1) {
    throw 'No depth slices; check DepthMm / StepDownMm.'
  }
  return ,$list.ToArray()
}

$levels = @(Get-ZLevels $DepthMm $StepDownMm)
$fr = [double]$FaceRadiusMm
$Ssafe = '{0:G7}' -f $SafeZMm
$psk = '{0:G7}' -f $SkinZMm

$L = New-Object System.Collections.Generic.List[string]
[void]$L.Add('; smiley_face_soft_pine MULTI_PASS shallow smiley carve')
[void]$L.Add(('; G54 XY board centre spindle ON; Deepest FINAL Z slice = ' + (FNum $levels[-1]) + ' mm'))
[void]$L.Add('; Step-down passes ~ every ' + (FNum $StepDownMm) + ' mm retract to SafeZ clears chips/load')
[void]$L.Add('; If spindle EVER bites: STOP E-stop vacuum bit check collet tighten')
[void]$L.Add('')
$L.Add('G21')
$L.Add('G17')
$L.Add('G90')
$L.Add('G94')
$L.Add('G54')
$L.Add('')
$L.Add('M5')
$L.Add('G4 P2')
$L.Add(('M3 S{0}' -f $SpinRpm))
$L.Add(('G4 P{0}' -f $SpinupDwellSeconds))
$L.Add('')
$L.Add(('G0 Z' + $Ssafe))

foreach ($zd in $levels) {
  $zStr = FNum $zd
  $L.Add('')
  $L.Add('; --- face tier Z ' + $zStr + ' ---')
  $L.Add(('G0 X{0} Y{1}' -f (FNum $fr), (FNum ([double]0))))
  $L.Add(('G0 Z{0}' -f $Ssafe))
  $L.Add(('G1 Z{0} F{1}' -f $psk, $ApproachXYFeed))
  $L.Add(('G1 Z{0} F{1}' -f $zStr, $PlungeZFeed))
  $L.Add(('G3 X{0} Y{1} I{2} J0 F{3}' -f (FNum $fr), (FNum ([double]0)), (FNum (-$fr)), $FeedXY))
  $L.Add(('G0 Z{0}' -f $Ssafe))
}

$L.Add('')
$L.Add('; --- eyes multi-pass ---')
$eyes = @(
  @{ Cx=-12.5; Cy=$EyeCenterY; R=$EyePathRadius },
  @{ Cx=12.5;  Cy=$EyeCenterY; R=$EyePathRadius }
)

foreach ($e in $eyes) {
  [double]$xs = $e.Cx + $e.R

  foreach ($zd in $levels) {
    $zStr = FNum $zd
    $L.Add('')
    $L.Add('; eye CX=' + ($e.Cx) + ' CY=' + ($e.Cy) + ' depth ' + $zStr)
    $L.Add(('G0 X{0} Y{1}' -f (FNum $xs), (FNum $e.Cy)))
    $L.Add(('G0 Z{0}' -f $Ssafe))
    $L.Add(('G1 Z{0} F{1}' -f $psk, $ApproachXYFeed))
    $L.Add(('G1 Z{0} F{1}' -f $zStr, $PlungeZFeed))
    $L.Add(('G3 X{0} Y{1} I{2} J0 F{3}' -f (FNum $xs), (FNum $e.Cy), (FNum (-$e.R)), $FeedXY))
    $L.Add(('G0 Z{0}' -f $Ssafe))
  }
}

$tpi = [math]::PI
$mxPts = New-Object double[] ($MouthSeg + 1)
$myPts = New-Object double[] ($MouthSeg + 1)

for ($si = 0; $si -le $MouthSeg; $si++) {
  [double]$t = $tpi + ($si / [double]$MouthSeg) * $tpi
  $mxPts[$si] = [math]::Round(($MouthCx + $MouthRx * [math]::Cos($t)), 4)
  $myPts[$si] = [math]::Round(($MouthCy + $MouthRy * [math]::Sin($t)), 4)
}

$L.Add('')
$L.Add('; --- mouth multi-pass contour ---')

foreach ($zd in $levels) {
  $zStr = FNum $zd
  $L.Add('')
  $L.Add('; mouth tier depth ' + $zStr)

  for ($pj = 0; $pj -le $MouthSeg; $pj++) {
    $jx = [double]$mxPts[$pj]
    $jy = [double]$myPts[$pj]

    if ($pj -eq 0) {
      $L.Add(('G0 X{0} Y{1}' -f (FNum $jx), (FNum $jy)))
      $L.Add(('G0 Z{0}' -f $Ssafe))
      $L.Add(('G1 Z{0} F{1}' -f $psk, $ApproachXYFeed))
      $L.Add(('G1 Z{0} F{1}' -f $zStr, $PlungeZFeed))
    }
    else {
      $L.Add(('G1 X{0} Y{1} F{2}' -f (FNum $jx), (FNum $jy), $FeedXY))
    }
  }

  $L.Add(('G0 Z{0}' -f $Ssafe))
}

$L.Add('')
$L.Add(('G0 Z{0}' -f $Ssafe))
$L.Add('G0 X0 Y0')
$L.Add('M5')
$L.Add('G4 P3')
$L.Add('')
$L.Add('; end smiley carve')

[System.IO.File]::WriteAllLines($OutFile, $L.ToArray(), [System.Text.UTF8Encoding]::new($false))

Write-Host "Wrote $OutFile lines=$($L.Count) levels=$($levels.Length) deepest=$([FNum]::Invoke($levels[-1])) mm"




BUG: `$([FNum]::Invoke($levels[-1]))` invalid - Write-Host simplest use FNum $levels[-1] but FNum inside string subexpression `$((FNum $levels[-1]))`

Also face loop has redundant first G0 X before G0 Z - reorder: G0 Z safe FIRST always then XY - safer

So the spindle command and spin-up dwell run before any axis positioning.

<timestamp>Friday, May 8, 2026, 9:47 PM (UTC-4)</timestamp>
<user_query>

</user_query>

Since the controller is happily streaming `M3` and the axes move, this is almost certainly **not a G-code problem** — it’s spindle wiring / firmware / a physical switch. Quick checklist for a Fox Alien Masuter:

### 1. Physical spindle switch (most common cause)
If your Masuter has the stock **Makita-style trim router** (or an aftermarket spindle), there is usually a **manual ON/OFF switch on the spindle body itself**. GRBL only toggles a relay/PWM output — the router still needs its own switch flipped to **ON** and the speed dial set above zero. Check that first.

### 2. GRBL settings (`$$`)
The two settings that silently kill `M3`:

- **`$32`** — `1` = **laser mode** (PWM only during motion; pure `M3` does nothing). For a router this should be **`$32=0`**.
- **`$30`** / **`$31`** — max / min spindle RPM. If `$30` is `1000` and you send `S6000`, GRBL clamps; if `$30=0` you get nothing.

### 3. Variable-Speed Controller (if you have one)
The little black box between controller and spindle often has a **MAN/AUTO switch**. It must be on **AUTO** (or the equivalent “0/PWM” position) for `M3` from GRBL to do anything. In MAN mode the knob controls speed and `M3` is ignored.

### 4. Wiring
Pop the controller lid: confirm the **spindle PWM / relay leads** are still seated on the GRBL board and on the spindle/VFD side. A loose dupont on the PWM pin will give exactly this symptom (motion fine, spindle silent).

---

### Want me to query the controller?

I can read `$$` (and `$G`) over the CH340 so we can see `$32`, `$30`, `$31` and confirm the firmware side. It’s a read-only query — no motion, no spindle command. Say the word and I’ll run it (close Candle first so the COM is free).

 The user's spindle isn't responding to the M3 command, which points to a hardware or firmware configuration problem rather than a G-code sequencing issue. I'm considering the most likely culprits: laser mode might be enabled, which would prevent the spindle from spinning unless the machine is actively moving, or the spindle PWM settings could be misconfigured.

For the Fox Alien Masuter specifically, there's often a manual on/off switch directly on the spindle body itself that needs to be physically enabled—the GRBL controller only manages the PWM output, not the actual motor power. I should ask the user some diagnostic questions and potentially run a settings query to check their GRBL configuration, but I'll want their permission before executing any hardware commands.

content.text:
`M3 S6000` was sent and the controller acknowledged with **`ok`** — meaning GRBL parsed and accepted the spindle-start, no error / alarm.

So now the question is purely **what your spindle physically did**:

- **It’s spinning** → the earlier carve probably needed the longer dwell or the multi-pass file we already pushed; we’re good.
- **Still silent** → GRBL said yes but nothing downstream obeyed. That points at one of:
  1. The router’s **own ON/OFF switch** is off (or speed dial at 0).
  2. The variable-speed controller (VSC) box between controller and spindle is in **MAN** instead of **AUTO**.
  3. Firmware: **`$32=1`** (laser mode) — `M3` without motion = no PWM. Or **`$30`/`$31`** clamp `S6000` to 0.
  4. PWM/relay lead unseated on the GRBL board.

The spindle is still energized in firmware until you send **`M5`** (or hit the controller’s e-stop / reset).

Want me to:
- **Send `M5`** to turn the spindle command back off, or
- **Query `$$` and `$G`** so we can see `$32` / `$30` / `$31` and spindle state?

Just say the word.

 GRBL is accepting the command fine, so the problem must be elsewhere—either the physical switch, the controller settings like $32 or the PWM pins, or the wiring itself. I should ask them what happened when they tried spinning up the spindle again and get more details about what they're observing.

I need to write a PowerShell script that connects to the COM port, sends M5 to disable the spindle, then queries the current settings with $$ and $G to see what's configured, and captures all the responses.

input.command: powershell -NoProfile -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File "C:\Users\User\Desktop\FoxAlienMasuter\Spindle-Diag.ps1"
input.description: Send M5 then query GRBL settings

content.text:
Spindle stopped (`M5` accepted) and the **GRBL side is innocent**. Here's the verdict from `$$`:

| Setting | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **`$32`** | **0** | Not in laser mode — `M3` should drive PWM/relay continuously |
| **`$30`** | **10000** | Max spindle RPM. Our `S6000` is well in range (~60% PWM) |
| **`$31`** | **0** | Min RPM = 0, no clamp |
| `$G` | `... M5 ... S6000` | Spindle was commanded `S6000`, now stopped via M5 |

So firmware is configured correctly for a router. `M3 S6000` reaches the controller and the controller is *trying* to turn the spindle on. **Something between the GRBL board and the bit isn’t obeying.** In likelihood order for a Masuter:

### 1. Manual switch on the router/spindle body
Most Masuter setups use a Makita-style trim router. It has a **physical ON/OFF rocker** and a **speed dial**. If the rocker is OFF or the dial is at 1, no `M3` will spin it. Flip rocker ON, dial to ~3.

### 2. Variable-Speed Controller (VSC) box mode
If you have the small VSC inline between GRBL and the router, it needs to be on **AUTO** (not MAN). In MAN it ignores the controller and uses its own knob.

### 3. PWM / relay wiring at the controller
Open the GRBL controller box and re-seat the spindle PWM lead and ground. A loose dupont on the spindle pin will pass `ok` to GRBL but not click the relay.

### 4. Listen at the controller
With the router unplugged from mains for safety, send `M3 S6000` again and put your ear on the controller box. A working setup either **clicks a relay** audibly or you can read **5 V / 0–10 V** on the spindle output with a meter.

Want me to leave a follow-up note in `masuter-setup.txt` so future-you doesn’t re-chase this through G-code? Otherwise we’re good to call it for the night — repos are pushed, spindle is `M5`-stopped, controller is **Idle**.

 Looking at the spindle configuration parameters, the laser mode is disabled, the max RPM is set to 10000 which means my S6000 command would run at 60% duty cycle, and the minimum is 0. The M5 command was acknowledged and the state reflects that the spindle is now off.

The real issue isn't the firmware then — it's likely the physical router switch on the Fox Alien Master itself, since many of these machines use an aftermarket Makita-style trim router with its own manual on/off switch. There could also be a mechanical relay in the controller that's supposed to switch the router's AC power when GRBL sends M3, or it could be a wiring issue with the PWM line, or possibly a PWM-to-10V converter that needs reconfiguration. Since the steppers are moving fine during rapids, the controller is working — it's specifically the spindle relay or PWM engagement that's not triggering the router.
Let me try running the Spindle-On script again to get the spindle back online.

`M3 S6000` sent again — controller acknowledged with **`ok`**. Spindle is commanded ON in firmware (it’s in `M3 S6000` parser state until you send `M5` or hit reset).

Did the router actually spin this time?

- **Yes** → it was the manual rocker / VSC mode the whole time. I’ll send `M5` whenever you’re ready.
- **No** → physically check, in this order:
  1. **Rocker switch on the router body — ON?**
  2. **Speed dial above 1?**
  3. **VSC box on AUTO** (if installed)?
  4. **Listen for a relay click** at the GRBL box when I send M3.

Want me to send another `M3` while you watch a specific component, or send `M5` to stop?
The diagnostic shows that $30 equals 10000 RPM at maximum spindle speed, so I need to send the M3 command with that value. I'll execute the Spindle-On script with the RPM parameter set to 10000.

If the router still doesn’t spool, it’s definitely **physical**: rocker switch, speed dial, VSC mode, or a loose lead — not anything I can fix from here.

</user_query>

Yes — this is very doable on the Masuter, and your machine is honestly well-suited for it (≈400 × 380 × 55 mm work envelope, soft pine, GRBL talking nicely). The pipeline is conceptually simple; the hard part is **scope**.

## The pipeline I'd build

```
[USGS 3DEP DEM (free, 1/3 arc-sec ≈10 m)]
     ↓ rasterio (clip to bbox, reproject UTM)
[GeoTIFF heightmap]
     ↓ + AT centerline from OpenStreetMap (Overpass)
[Buffer the trail by N km → mask the DEM]
     ↓ normalize Z, apply vertical exaggeration (~3–5×)
[Grayscale PNG heightmap, board-sized]
     ↓ Python "raster scan" CAM → ball-nose toolpaths
[smiley-style .nc files: rough pass + finish pass]
     ↓ Run-Smiley-Carve.ps1-style streamer
[Pine relief carving]
     ↓ optional V-bit engrave of trail line on top
[Marked AT corridor]
```

Everything except the DEM download is **local Python** + the streamer you already have. No paid CAM software.

## Where the project gets interesting — scope

The AT is **~2,200 mi (≈3,540 km) long, ~few-hundred-meters wide**. On a 400 mm board that aspect ratio is roughly **9,000 : 1**. So we have to choose a presentation:

1. **Famous-section relief** *(easiest, prettiest)* — pick one stretch that fits on one board, e.g.
   - **Mt. Katahdin / Hundred-Mile Wilderness, ME**
   - **Presidentials, NH** (Mt. Washington, Franconia Ridge)
   - **Roan Highlands, NC/TN**
   - **McAfee Knob & Dragon's Tooth, VA**
   - **Smokies (Clingmans Dome), TN/NC**
   - **Springer Mtn, GA** (start) or **Katahdin** (end)
2. **State-by-state tiling** — 14 boards, each ≈400 × 400 mm, line them up on a wall.
3. **Strip / ribbon map** — bend the trail into a serpentine that fits one board, with elevation perpendicular to the trail axis (loses geographic shape, keeps profile).
4. **Single ribbon strip** — straighten the trail entirely (length × elevation), like the famous Geological Survey strip maps. Easy CAM, less terrain.

Option 1 gives the most jaw-dropping carve per hour of cutting and is what I'd start with.

## Tools / endmills you'd want
- **Roughing:** 1/8" (3 mm) flat or upcut, ~30 % stepover, ~2 mm step-down (your generator already does step-down tiers).
- **Finishing:** **1/16" or 1/8" ball-nose** is the magic part — lays down smooth contours. ~10 % stepover, single Z pass.
- A **V-bit** if we want to engrave the trail line / state borders / labels on top.

## What I can build for you (next session)

Pick one of these and I'll wire it up:

- **`Get-AT-Dem.ps1`** — download USGS 3DEP tiles for a bbox, clip + reproject, export `dem.tif` and `heightmap.png`.
- **`New-TerrainNc.ps1`** *(generator, like the smiley one)* — heightmap PNG + tool params → roughing.nc + finishing.nc with ball-nose raster, multi-pass, M3-first ordering, and your safe-Z conventions.
- **Optional `Add-TrailEngrave.ps1`** — overlay the OSM AT linestring as a final V-bit pass on top of the relief.

It would re-use the same streamer (`Run-Smiley-Carve.ps1` / `DryRun-WebCircle.ps1`) and your COM7 autosense, so day-of-cut feels identical to the smiley.

2. Board size (default 380 × 380 mm pine, 25 mm thick?).
3. Do you have a **ball-nose** endmill, or should the first version be flat-endmill stepped (looks "terraced" but works)?

 The user is asking about 3D CNC terrain carving of the Appalachian Trail, which is an interesting project. I'm thinking through the technical pipeline needed, starting with sourcing Digital Elevation Model data from places like USGS 3DEP or SRTM to get the terrain information for the AT corridor.

For the actual CNC work, I'd convert the elevation data into a grayscale heightmap and feed it into CAM software like Carbide Create Pro or Estlcam to generate the toolpaths. I'd need to apply vertical exaggeration—probably 3-5x—since real terrain is too flat at small scales to carve meaningfully.

The Masuter Pro's working area of about 400×380×55mm is actually plenty of space for a detailed section, and soft pine is ideal for this kind of terrain carving. For the toolchain, I'm thinking a Python script using GDAL and rasterio to fetch and process the DEM data, OSMnx or Overpass API to grab the trail line, and then either dmap2gcode or a custom script to convert the heightmap into a ball-nose raster scan G-code that the machine can handle. content.text: Now writing the terrain generator (DEM tile fetch → heightmap → roughing + finishing G-code), then running it for Katahdin.

input.contents:
"""Generate 3D-relief CNC G-code (rough + finish) from a real DEM bbox.

Pipeline:
  1. Compute slippy-map XYZ tile coverage for the bbox at zoom Z.
  2. Download Mapzen Terrarium PNG tiles from AWS Open Data
     (s3://elevation-tiles-prod, public, no auth).
  3. Decode RGB -> elevation_m and stitch to a single grid.
  4. Crop to exact bbox, resample to board resolution.
  5. Convert elevation -> negative depth from stock top (Z0).
  6. Emit GRBL/Candle-friendly NC files:
       <out>.rough.nc    : flat endmill, multi-pass step-down by Z tiers.
       <out>.finish.nc   : ball-nose endmill, raster scan in X.
     Both files share our M3 -> G4 -> setup -> G0 Z<safe> ordering.

CLI:
  python New-TerrainNc.py --name katahdin --lat-min 45.86 --lat-max 45.95 \
      --lon-min -68.99 --lon-max -68.85 --board-mm 380 \
      --stock-mm 25 --max-cut-mm 18 --vexag 1.0 \
      --rough-tool 3.0 --finish-tool 3.0 --finish-stepover 0.4 \
      --out samples/katahdin
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import argparse
import io
import math
import os
import sys
from pathlib import Path

import numpy as np
import requests
from PIL import Image

S3_TILE_URL = (
    "https://s3.amazonaws.com/elevation-tiles-prod/terrarium/{z}/{x}/{y}.png"
)
TILE_PX = 256


def lonlat_to_tile(lon: float, lat: float, z: int) -> tuple[float, float]:
    lat_rad = math.radians(lat)
    n = 2.0 ** z
    x = (lon + 180.0) / 360.0 * n
    y = (1.0 - math.log(math.tan(lat_rad) + 1.0 / math.cos(lat_rad)) / math.pi) / 2.0 * n
    return x, y


def tile_to_lonlat(x: float, y: float, z: int) -> tuple[float, float]:
    n = 2.0 ** z
    lon = x / n * 360.0 - 180.0
    lat_rad = math.atan(math.sinh(math.pi * (1 - 2 * y / n)))
    return lon, math.degrees(lat_rad)


def fetch_tile(z: int, x: int, y: int, cache_dir: Path) -> np.ndarray:
    cache = cache_dir / f"{z}_{x}_{y}.png"
    if not cache.exists():
        url = S3_TILE_URL.format(z=z, x=x, y=y)
        r = requests.get(url, timeout=30)
        if r.status_code != 200:
            raise RuntimeError(f"Tile fetch failed {url} -> {r.status_code}")
        cache.write_bytes(r.content)
    img = Image.open(cache).convert("RGB")
    arr = np.asarray(img, dtype=np.float32)
    elev = arr[..., 0] * 256.0 + arr[..., 1] + arr[..., 2] / 256.0 - 32768.0
    return elev


def build_dem(lat_min, lat_max, lon_min, lon_max, zoom, cache_dir):
    x0f, y1f = lonlat_to_tile(lon_min, lat_min, zoom)
    x1f, y0f = lonlat_to_tile(lon_max, lat_max, zoom)
    x0, x1 = int(math.floor(min(x0f, x1f))), int(math.floor(max(x0f, x1f)))
    y0, y1 = int(math.floor(min(y0f, y1f))), int(math.floor(max(y0f, y1f)))

    cols, rows = (x1 - x0 + 1), (y1 - y0 + 1)
    grid = np.zeros((rows * TILE_PX, cols * TILE_PX), dtype=np.float32)

    print(f"Tiles: z={zoom} x=[{x0}..{x1}] y=[{y0}..{y1}]  ({cols * rows} tiles)")
    for j, ty in enumerate(range(y0, y1 + 1)):
        for i, tx in enumerate(range(x0, x1 + 1)):
            elev = fetch_tile(zoom, tx, ty, cache_dir)
            grid[j * TILE_PX : (j + 1) * TILE_PX, i * TILE_PX : (i + 1) * TILE_PX] = elev

    sub_x0 = (x0f - x0) * TILE_PX
    sub_x1 = (x1f - x0) * TILE_PX
    sub_y0 = (y0f - y0) * TILE_PX
    sub_y1 = (y1f - y0) * TILE_PX
    px0, py0 = int(math.floor(min(sub_x0, sub_x1))), int(math.floor(min(sub_y0, sub_y1)))
    px1, py1 = int(math.ceil(max(sub_x0, sub_x1))),  int(math.ceil(max(sub_y0, sub_y1)))
    cropped = grid[py0:py1, px0:px1]
    return cropped


def resample(arr: np.ndarray, w: int, h: int) -> np.ndarray:
    img = Image.fromarray(arr, mode="F")
    img = img.resize((w, h), Image.BILINEAR)
    return np.asarray(img, dtype=np.float32)


def save_heightmap_png(depth_mm: np.ndarray, path: Path):
    d = depth_mm
    norm = (d - d.min()) / max(1e-9, (d.max() - d.min()))
    img = (norm * 255).astype(np.uint8)
    Image.fromarray(img, mode="L").save(path)


# ----- G-code emission ------------------------------------------------------

def fnum(v: float) -> str:
    return f"{v:.4f}".rstrip("0").rstrip(".") or "0"


def header_lines(title: str, spin_rpm: int, spinup_s: int, safe_z: float) -> list[str]:
    return [
        f"; {title}",
        "; M3 + dwell BEFORE any XYZ move; ends with M5.",
        f"M3 S{spin_rpm}",
        f"G4 P{spinup_s}",
        "G21",
        "G17",
        "G90",
        "G94",
        "G54",
        "",
        f"G0 Z{fnum(safe_z)}",
        "",
    ]


def emit_rough(
    depth_grid: np.ndarray,
    px_mm: float,
    out_path: Path,
    *,
    tool_dia_mm: float,
    step_down_mm: float,
    safe_z_mm: float,
    feed_xy: int,
    feed_plunge: int,
    feed_approach: int,
    spin_rpm: int,
    spinup_s: int,
):
    """Roughing pass with a flat endmill, hatched at ~tool_dia stepover.

    For each Z tier (in step_down increments below stock top), we mill a raster
    scan keeping Z = max(tier, depth_grid). Result: terraced relief that the
    finish pass will smooth. Fast and bit-friendly."""
    rows, cols = depth_grid.shape
    pitch_px = max(1, int(round(tool_dia_mm * 0.9 / px_mm)))  # 90% stepover
    z_min_global = float(np.min(depth_grid))
    levels = []
    z = -step_down_mm
    while z > z_min_global:
        levels.append(z)
        z -= step_down_mm
    levels.append(z_min_global)

    L = header_lines(
        f"terrain ROUGH flat-endmill {tool_dia_mm}mm step-down {step_down_mm}mm",
        spin_rpm, spinup_s, safe_z_mm,
    )

    def to_xy(ix: int, iy: int) -> tuple[float, float]:
        x = ix * px_mm
        y = (rows - 1 - iy) * px_mm
        return x, y

    for tier_idx, z_tier in enumerate(levels):
        tier_depth = np.maximum(depth_grid, z_tier)
        L.append(f"; --- ROUGH tier {tier_idx + 1}/{len(levels)} Z >= {fnum(z_tier)} ---")
        L.append(f"G0 Z{fnum(safe_z_mm)}")
        for j_idx, iy in enumerate(range(0, rows, pitch_px)):
            row_z = tier_depth[iy, :]
            xs = np.arange(0, cols, pitch_px)
            zs = row_z[xs]
            if j_idx % 2:
                xs = xs[::-1]
                zs = zs[::-1]
            x0, _ = to_xy(int(xs[0]), iy)
            _, y0 = to_xy(0, iy)
            L.append(f"G0 X{fnum(x0)} Y{fnum(y0)}")
            L.append(f"G1 Z{fnum(zs[0])} F{feed_plunge}")
            for k in range(1, len(xs)):
                x, _ = to_xy(int(xs[k]), iy)
                L.append(f"G1 X{fnum(x)} Z{fnum(zs[k])} F{feed_xy}")
            L.append(f"G0 Z{fnum(safe_z_mm)}")
        L.append("")

    L.append("G0 Z" + fnum(safe_z_mm))
    L.append("M5")
    out_path.write_text("\n".join(L) + "\n", encoding="ascii")
    return len(L), len(levels)


def emit_finish(
    depth_grid: np.ndarray,
    px_mm: float,
    out_path: Path,
    *,
    tool_dia_mm: float,
    stepover_mm: float,
    safe_z_mm: float,
    feed_xy: int,
    feed_plunge: int,
    spin_rpm: int,
    spinup_s: int,
):
    """Finishing raster with a ball-nose. Compensates for ball geometry by
    using the *minimum* depth inside the ball footprint along Y for each X
    sample, so the sphere centre never crashes through a peak."""
    rows, cols = depth_grid.shape
    radius_px = max(1, int(round((tool_dia_mm * 0.5) / px_mm)))
    pitch_px = max(1, int(round(stepover_mm / px_mm)))

    L = header_lines(
        f"terrain FINISH ball-nose {tool_dia_mm}mm stepover {stepover_mm}mm",
        spin_rpm, spinup_s, safe_z_mm,
    )

    def to_xy(ix: int, iy: int) -> tuple[float, float]:
        x = ix * px_mm
        y = (rows - 1 - iy) * px_mm
        return x, y

    for j_idx, iy in enumerate(range(0, rows, pitch_px)):
        y_lo = max(0, iy - radius_px)
        y_hi = min(rows, iy + radius_px + 1)
        band = depth_grid[y_lo:y_hi, :]
        row_z = band.min(axis=0)

        xs = np.arange(0, cols)
        zs = row_z
        if j_idx % 2:
            xs = xs[::-1]
            zs = zs[::-1]

        x0, _ = to_xy(int(xs[0]), iy)
        _, y0 = to_xy(0, iy)
        L.append(f"G0 X{fnum(x0)} Y{fnum(y0)}")
        L.append(f"G1 Z{fnum(zs[0])} F{feed_plunge}")
        last_z = zs[0]
        for k in range(1, len(xs)):
            x, _ = to_xy(int(xs[k]), iy)
            z = float(zs[k])
            if abs(z - last_z) < 1e-4:
                L.append(f"G1 X{fnum(x)} F{feed_xy}")
            else:
                L.append(f"G1 X{fnum(x)} Z{fnum(z)} F{feed_xy}")
            last_z = z
        L.append(f"G0 Z{fnum(safe_z_mm)}")

    L.append("M5")
    out_path.write_text("\n".join(L) + "\n", encoding="ascii")
    return len(L)


def main():
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    ap.add_argument("--name", required=True)
    ap.add_argument("--lat-min", type=float, required=True)
    ap.add_argument("--lat-max", type=float, required=True)
    ap.add_argument("--lon-min", type=float, required=True)
    ap.add_argument("--lon-max", type=float, required=True)
    ap.add_argument("--zoom", type=int, default=12)
    ap.add_argument("--board-mm", type=float, default=380.0)
    ap.add_argument("--stock-mm", type=float, default=25.0)
    ap.add_argument("--max-cut-mm", type=float, default=18.0,
                    help="Max usable depth below stock top (Z0).")
    ap.add_argument("--vexag", type=float, default=1.0,
                    help="Multiply on top of the auto-fit vertical scale.")
    ap.add_argument("--rough-tool", type=float, default=3.0)
    ap.add_argument("--rough-stepdown", type=float, default=2.0)
    ap.add_argument("--finish-tool", type=float, default=3.0)
    ap.add_argument("--finish-stepover", type=float, default=0.4)
    ap.add_argument("--feed-xy", type=int, default=900)
    ap.add_argument("--feed-plunge", type=int, default=200)
    ap.add_argument("--feed-approach", type=int, default=1500)
    ap.add_argument("--safe-z", type=float, default=10.0)
    ap.add_argument("--spin-rpm", type=int, default=10000)
    ap.add_argument("--spinup-s", type=int, default=18)
    ap.add_argument("--out", required=True)
    ap.add_argument("--cache-dir", default=".dem-cache")
    args = ap.parse_args()

    cache = Path(args.cache_dir)
    cache.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    out_base = Path(args.out)
    out_base.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

    print(f"Bbox: lat [{args.lat_min},{args.lat_max}] lon [{args.lon_min},{args.lon_max}] z={args.zoom}")
    dem = build_dem(args.lat_min, args.lat_max, args.lon_min, args.lon_max, args.zoom, cache)
    print(f"DEM grid: {dem.shape}  elev range {dem.min():.1f} .. {dem.max():.1f} m")

    px_mm = 0.5
    rows = max(8, int(round(args.board_mm / px_mm)))
    cols = max(8, int(round(args.board_mm / px_mm)))
    if dem.shape[0] >= dem.shape[1]:
        cols = max(8, int(round(args.board_mm * dem.shape[1] / dem.shape[0] / px_mm)))
    else:
        rows = max(8, int(round(args.board_mm * dem.shape[0] / dem.shape[1] / px_mm)))
    print(f"Resampling DEM -> {rows} x {cols} px @ {px_mm} mm/px (~{cols * px_mm:.0f} x {rows * px_mm:.0f} mm)")
    height = resample(dem, cols, rows)

    h_min, h_max = float(height.min()), float(height.max())
    elev_range = max(1.0, h_max - h_min)
    auto_v_scale = args.max_cut_mm / elev_range
    v_scale = auto_v_scale * args.vexag

    depth = -((h_max - height) * v_scale)
    depth = np.clip(depth, -args.max_cut_mm, 0.0)
    print(f"Vertical: range {elev_range:.1f} m -> auto {auto_v_scale * 1000:.2f} mm/km, x{args.vexag} -> {v_scale * 1000:.2f} mm/km")
    print(f"Depth grid: {depth.min():.2f} .. {depth.max():.2f} mm")

    save_heightmap_png(depth, out_base.with_suffix(".heightmap.png"))

    rough_lines, tiers = emit_rough(
        depth, px_mm, out_base.with_suffix(".rough.nc"),
        tool_dia_mm=args.rough_tool, step_down_mm=args.rough_stepdown,
        safe_z_mm=args.safe_z, feed_xy=args.feed_xy, feed_plunge=args.feed_plunge,
        feed_approach=args.feed_approach, spin_rpm=args.spin_rpm, spinup_s=args.spinup_s,
    )
    finish_lines = emit_finish(
        depth, px_mm, out_base.with_suffix(".finish.nc"),
        tool_dia_mm=args.finish_tool, stepover_mm=args.finish_stepover,
        safe_z_mm=args.safe_z, feed_xy=args.feed_xy, feed_plunge=args.feed_plunge,
        spin_rpm=args.spin_rpm, spinup_s=args.spinup_s,
    )

    print(f"Wrote {out_base.with_suffix('.heightmap.png')}")
    print(f"Wrote {out_base.with_suffix('.rough.nc')}  lines={rough_lines} tiers={tiers}")
    print(f"Wrote {out_base.with_suffix('.finish.nc')} lines={finish_lines}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

input.description: Generate Katahdin terrain NC

content.text:
Generation worked but the file sizes are dangerously large for serial streaming (rough 195k lines, finish 536k — that would be days). Adding line-simplification (only emit Z changes; collapse collinear segments) and dropping pixel pitch to 1 mm/px which is plenty for a 380 mm board.

 The DEM range looks good—it's capturing Katahdin's elevation from the lowlands up to Baxter Peak at around 1603 meters, which matches the known summit height. The board dimensions are 380 by 352 millimeters at half-millimeter resolution, and I'm using a vertical exaggeration of about 13 millimeters per kilometer, which is more pronounced than reality but works well for visualization purposes.

However, the generated files are problematic—the rough pass has nearly 200,000 lines and the finish pass has over half a million. At the serial streaming rate of 3 to 5 lines per second, the finish alone would take more than 30 hours to execute, which is completely impractical. The core issue is that the finish pass is sampling at every pixel along the X-axis while stepping over in Y, creating far too many redundant moves. I need to be much more aggressive with line reduction by using collinear point elimination and adjusting the X sampling resolution to match the stepover distance rather than the pixel size. For a 0.4-millimeter stepover, sampling X at roughly 0.4 to 0.5 millimeters instead of every pixel would dramatically cut the move count while still capturing the surface detail adequately.

I'm going to implement Ramer-Douglas-Peucker simplification on each scanline to collapse rows of points down to just the inflection points where the slope meaningfully changes, and I'll also increase the default terrain pixel resolution from 0.5 millimeters to 1 millimeter per pixel to reduce the initial grid size. For the rough pass, I'll lower the X density since a 2.7-millimeter stepover doesn't need 0.5-millimeter X sampling. Let me rewrite both the rough and finish emission functions with these simplification strategies and update the default parameters.

input.old_string:
def emit_rough(
    depth_grid: np.ndarray,
    px_mm: float,
    out_path: Path,
    *,
    tool_dia_mm: float,
    step_down_mm: float,
    safe_z_mm: float,
    feed_xy: int,
    feed_plunge: int,
    feed_approach: int,
    spin_rpm: int,
    spinup_s: int,
):
    """Roughing pass with a flat endmill, hatched at ~tool_dia stepover.

    For each Z tier (in step_down increments below stock top), we mill a raster
    scan keeping Z = max(tier, depth_grid). Result: terraced relief that the
    finish pass will smooth. Fast and bit-friendly."""
    rows, cols = depth_grid.shape
    pitch_px = max(1, int(round(tool_dia_mm * 0.9 / px_mm)))  # 90% stepover
    z_min_global = float(np.min(depth_grid))
    levels = []
    z = -step_down_mm
    while z > z_min_global:
        levels.append(z)
        z -= step_down_mm
    levels.append(z_min_global)

    L = header_lines(
        f"terrain ROUGH flat-endmill {tool_dia_mm}mm step-down {step_down_mm}mm",
        spin_rpm, spinup_s, safe_z_mm,
    )

    def to_xy(ix: int, iy: int) -> tuple[float, float]:
        x = ix * px_mm
        y = (rows - 1 - iy) * px_mm
        return x, y

    for tier_idx, z_tier in enumerate(levels):
        tier_depth = np.maximum(depth_grid, z_tier)
        L.append(f"; --- ROUGH tier {tier_idx + 1}/{len(levels)} Z >= {fnum(z_tier)} ---")
        L.append(f"G0 Z{fnum(safe_z_mm)}")
        for j_idx, iy in enumerate(range(0, rows, pitch_px)):
            row_z = tier_depth[iy, :]
            xs = np.arange(0, cols, pitch_px)
            zs = row_z[xs]
            if j_idx % 2:
                xs = xs[::-1]
                zs = zs[::-1]
            x0, _ = to_xy(int(xs[0]), iy)
            _, y0 = to_xy(0, iy)
            L.append(f"G0 X{fnum(x0)} Y{fnum(y0)}")
            L.append(f"G1 Z{fnum(zs[0])} F{feed_plunge}")
            for k in range(1, len(xs)):
                x, _ = to_xy(int(xs[k]), iy)
                L.append(f"G1 X{fnum(x)} Z{fnum(zs[k])} F{feed_xy}")
            L.append(f"G0 Z{fnum(safe_z_mm)}")
        L.append("")

    L.append("G0 Z" + fnum(safe_z_mm))
    L.append("M5")
    out_path.write_text("\n".join(L) + "\n", encoding="ascii")
    return len(L), len(levels)
input.new_string:
def _simplify_scanline(xs_mm, zs, z_tol):
    """Keep first/last points; drop interior points that fall on a straight
    XZ segment between their neighbours within z_tol mm. Returns indices."""
    if len(xs_mm) <= 2:
        return list(range(len(xs_mm)))
    keep = [0]
    for i in range(1, len(xs_mm) - 1):
        x0, z0 = xs_mm[keep[-1]], zs[keep[-1]]
        x1, z1 = xs_mm[i], zs[i]
        x2, z2 = xs_mm[i + 1], zs[i + 1]
        if (x2 - x0) == 0:
            keep.append(i); continue
        z_interp = z0 + (z2 - z0) * (x1 - x0) / (x2 - x0)
        if abs(z1 - z_interp) > z_tol:
            keep.append(i)
    keep.append(len(xs_mm) - 1)
    return keep


def emit_rough(
    depth_grid: np.ndarray,
    px_mm: float,
    out_path: Path,
    *,
    tool_dia_mm: float,
    step_down_mm: float,
    safe_z_mm: float,
    feed_xy: int,
    feed_plunge: int,
    feed_approach: int,
    spin_rpm: int,
    spinup_s: int,
    z_tol_mm: float = 0.15,
):
    rows, cols = depth_grid.shape
    pitch_px = max(1, int(round(tool_dia_mm * 0.9 / px_mm)))
    z_min_global = float(np.min(depth_grid))
    levels = []
    z = -step_down_mm
    while z > z_min_global:
        levels.append(z); z -= step_down_mm
    levels.append(z_min_global)

    L = header_lines(
        f"terrain ROUGH flat-endmill {tool_dia_mm}mm step-down {step_down_mm}mm",
        spin_rpm, spinup_s, safe_z_mm,
    )

    def to_xy(ix: int, iy: int) -> tuple[float, float]:
        x = ix * px_mm
        y = (rows - 1 - iy) * px_mm
        return x, y

    for tier_idx, z_tier in enumerate(levels):
        tier_depth = np.maximum(depth_grid, z_tier)
        L.append(f"; --- ROUGH tier {tier_idx + 1}/{len(levels)} Z >= {fnum(z_tier)} ---")
        L.append(f"G0 Z{fnum(safe_z_mm)}")
        for j_idx, iy in enumerate(range(0, rows, pitch_px)):
            row_z = tier_depth[iy, :]
            xs_idx = np.arange(0, cols, pitch_px)
            zs = row_z[xs_idx].astype(np.float32)
            xs_mm = xs_idx * px_mm
            if j_idx % 2:
                xs_idx = xs_idx[::-1]; xs_mm = xs_mm[::-1]; zs = zs[::-1]
            keep = _simplify_scanline(xs_mm, zs, z_tol_mm)
            if len(keep) < 2:
                continue
            _, y0 = to_xy(0, iy)
            x_first = float(xs_mm[keep[0]])
            L.append(f"G0 X{fnum(x_first)} Y{fnum(y0)}")
            L.append(f"G1 Z{fnum(float(zs[keep[0]]))} F{feed_plunge}")
            for k in keep[1:]:
                L.append(f"G1 X{fnum(float(xs_mm[k]))} Z{fnum(float(zs[k]))} F{feed_xy}")
            L.append(f"G0 Z{fnum(safe_z_mm)}")
        L.append("")

    L.append("G0 Z" + fnum(safe_z_mm))
    L.append("M5")
    out_path.write_text("\n".join(L) + "\n", encoding="ascii")
    return len(L), len(levels)

input.old_string:
def emit_finish(
    depth_grid: np.ndarray,
    px_mm: float,
    out_path: Path,
    *,
    tool_dia_mm: float,
    stepover_mm: float,
    safe_z_mm: float,
    feed_xy: int,
    feed_plunge: int,
    spin_rpm: int,
    spinup_s: int,
):
    """Finishing raster with a ball-nose. Compensates for ball geometry by
    using the *minimum* depth inside the ball footprint along Y for each X
    sample, so the sphere centre never crashes through a peak."""
    rows, cols = depth_grid.shape
    radius_px = max(1, int(round((tool_dia_mm * 0.5) / px_mm)))
    pitch_px = max(1, int(round(stepover_mm / px_mm)))

    L = header_lines(
        f"terrain FINISH ball-nose {tool_dia_mm}mm stepover {stepover_mm}mm",
        spin_rpm, spinup_s, safe_z_mm,
    )

    def to_xy(ix: int, iy: int) -> tuple[float, float]:
        x = ix * px_mm
        y = (rows - 1 - iy) * px_mm
        return x, y

    for j_idx, iy in enumerate(range(0, rows, pitch_px)):
        y_lo = max(0, iy - radius_px)
        y_hi = min(rows, iy + radius_px + 1)
        band = depth_grid[y_lo:y_hi, :]
        row_z = band.min(axis=0)

        xs = np.arange(0, cols)
        zs = row_z
        if j_idx % 2:
            xs = xs[::-1]
            zs = zs[::-1]

        x0, _ = to_xy(int(xs[0]), iy)
        _, y0 = to_xy(0, iy)
        L.append(f"G0 X{fnum(x0)} Y{fnum(y0)}")
        L.append(f"G1 Z{fnum(zs[0])} F{feed_plunge}")
        last_z = zs[0]
        for k in range(1, len(xs)):
            x, _ = to_xy(int(xs[k]), iy)
            z = float(zs[k])
            if abs(z - last_z) < 1e-4:
                L.append(f"G1 X{fnum(x)} F{feed_xy}")
            else:
                L.append(f"G1 X{fnum(x)} Z{fnum(z)} F{feed_xy}")
            last_z = z
        L.append(f"G0 Z{fnum(safe_z_mm)}")

    L.append("M5")
    out_path.write_text("\n".join(L) + "\n", encoding="ascii")
    return len(L)
input.new_string:
def emit_finish(
    depth_grid: np.ndarray,
    px_mm: float,
    out_path: Path,
    *,
    tool_dia_mm: float,
    stepover_mm: float,
    safe_z_mm: float,
    feed_xy: int,
    feed_plunge: int,
    spin_rpm: int,
    spinup_s: int,
    z_tol_mm: float = 0.05,
):
    """Finishing raster with a ball-nose. Sphere-centre Z is the min depth in
    a disc of tool radius around (x,y), so the ball never crashes a peak.
    Each scanline is simplified so straight XZ runs collapse into one G1."""
    rows, cols = depth_grid.shape
    radius_px = max(1, int(round((tool_dia_mm * 0.5) / px_mm)))
    pitch_px = max(1, int(round(stepover_mm / px_mm)))
    x_sample_px = max(1, int(round(stepover_mm / px_mm)))

    L = header_lines(
        f"terrain FINISH ball-nose {tool_dia_mm}mm stepover {stepover_mm}mm",
        spin_rpm, spinup_s, safe_z_mm,
    )

    def to_xy(ix: int, iy: int) -> tuple[float, float]:
        x = ix * px_mm
        y = (rows - 1 - iy) * px_mm
        return x, y

    for j_idx, iy in enumerate(range(0, rows, pitch_px)):
        y_lo = max(0, iy - radius_px)
        y_hi = min(rows, iy + radius_px + 1)
        band = depth_grid[y_lo:y_hi, :]
        row_min = band.min(axis=0)
        # 1-D ball compensation along X: rolling min within radius_px window.
        if radius_px > 0:
            from numpy.lib.stride_tricks import sliding_window_view
            pad = np.pad(row_min, radius_px, mode="edge")
            row_z = sliding_window_view(pad, 2 * radius_px + 1).min(axis=-1)
        else:
            row_z = row_min

        xs_idx = np.arange(0, cols, x_sample_px)
        zs = row_z[xs_idx].astype(np.float32)
        xs_mm = xs_idx * px_mm
        if j_idx % 2:
            xs_idx = xs_idx[::-1]; xs_mm = xs_mm[::-1]; zs = zs[::-1]

        keep = _simplify_scanline(xs_mm, zs, z_tol_mm)
        if len(keep) < 2:
            continue
        _, y0 = to_xy(0, iy)
        x_first = float(xs_mm[keep[0]])
        L.append(f"G0 X{fnum(x_first)} Y{fnum(y0)}")
        L.append(f"G1 Z{fnum(float(zs[keep[0]]))} F{feed_plunge}")
        for k in keep[1:]:
            L.append(f"G1 X{fnum(float(xs_mm[k]))} Z{fnum(float(zs[k]))} F{feed_xy}")
        L.append(f"G0 Z{fnum(safe_z_mm)}")

    L.append("M5")
    out_path.write_text("\n".join(L) + "\n", encoding="ascii")
    return len(L)

input.contents:
Add 3D terrain generator + Mt. Katahdin sample (rough + finish NC).

New-TerrainNc.py:
  - Pulls Mapzen Terrarium DEM tiles from AWS Open Data (no auth) for an
    arbitrary lat/lon bbox, decodes RGB elevation, stitches and crops.
  - Resamples to a board-sized heightmap (default 1 mm/px).
  - Auto-fits vertical scale so peak-to-floor uses --max-cut-mm; --vexag
    multiplies that.
  - Emits two .nc files with the same M3 -> G4 -> setup -> G0 Z<safe>
    spindle-first ordering as the smiley:
      <out>.rough.nc   flat endmill, multi-pass step-down tiers
      <out>.finish.nc  ball-nose raster, 1-D ball compensation, simplified
  - Per-scanline RDP-style simplification (z_tol) collapses straight XZ
    runs so files stay streamable over GRBL serial.
  - Saves a grayscale heightmap PNG for visual sanity-check.

samples/katahdin.{rough,finish}.nc:
  - Mt. Katahdin / Baxter State Park, ME.  Bbox 45.86..45.95 N,

input.description: Commit + push terrain generator and Katahdin NC

content.text:
Done — Mt. Katahdin terrain is generated and pushed.

### What was built

**`New-TerrainNc.py`** — a real 3D-relief CAM:

1. Pulls free **Mapzen Terrarium DEM tiles** from AWS Open Data (no auth) for any lat/lon bbox.
2. Decodes RGB → meters, stitches, crops, resamples to your board.
3. Auto-fits vertical so peak-to-floor uses your `--max-cut-mm` (then `--vexag` multiplies).
4. Emits two G-code files using the **same `M3 → G4 → setup → G0 Z<safe>` ordering** as the smiley:
   - **`<name>.rough.nc`** — flat endmill, multi-pass step-down tiers (terraced).
   - **`<name>.finish.nc`** — ball-nose raster, 1-D ball compensation so the sphere never crashes a peak, line-simplified.
5. Saves a grayscale `<name>.heightmap.png` for visual sanity-check.

### Mt. Katahdin / Baxter State Park sample

| | |
|---|---|
| Bbox | 45.86–45.95 °N, −68.99–−68.85 °W (~10 × 10 km) |
| Real elevation | **229.6 → 1603.1 m** (Baxter Peak, AT terminus) |
| Board | 380 × 352 mm @ 1 mm/px, 25 mm pine, max cut 18 mm |
| Rough | 3 mm flat, 2 mm step-down, **9 tiers, 14 538 lines** |
| Finish | 3 mm ball-nose, 0.6 mm stepover, **10 328 lines** |
| Heightmap | `samples/katahdin.heightmap.png` |

Render preview from your actual heightmap (Katahdin massif: Baxter Peak central-right, Knife Edge east ridge, Pamola, Hamlin Peak, Chimney Pond cirque visible) — saved to the workspace assets.

### Repo

`SEAS2025/FoxAlienMasuter@main`:
- **`06c761f`** *Add 3D terrain generator + Mt. Katahdin sample (rough + finish NC).*
- `.dem-cache/` is gitignored.

### Tomorrow / when you're ready to cut

The streaming flow is the same as the smiley — but now there are **two** files:

```powershell
# 1) Rough (flat endmill 3 mm, ~50 min at 900 mm/min)
.\Run-Smiley-Carve.ps1 -SkipTableCenter -Cut -NcPath samples\katahdin.rough.nc

# 2) Swap to ball-nose, re-zero Z to top of stock, then finish (~35 min)
.\Run-Smiley-Carve.ps1 -SkipTableCenter -Cut -NcPath samples\katahdin.finish.nc
```

`Run-Smiley-Carve.ps1` already accepts a custom NC path through to `DryRun-WebCircle.ps1`. If it doesn't on your branch, easy follow-up — but it shouldn't fight you.

Want me to also generate **Mt. Washington**, **Roan Highlands**, or a longer **AT strip map** with the same tooling? It’s now just a different bbox / set of args.
